coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-doped g-C3N4 nanocomposites with enhanced piezoelectric catalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Changwang
Zhao, Zhong
Jin, Wanhui
Yu, Qian
Yu, Jing
Ran, Jianhua
Bi, Shuguang
Cheng, Deshan
Li, Daiqi
Cai, Guangming
Wang, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Construction of charge transfer channel is a feasible way to enhance efficiency in catalysis applications. In this study, hydrothermally synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were deposited on g-C3N4 nanosheets via the coprecipitation calcination method to couple the piezoelectrical nature of g-C3N4 with the prominent charge transfer capability of CQDs, resulting in enhanced piezo-catalytic performance of the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Specifically, a 92.5% methylene blue degradation rate was achieved after constant stirring of the methylene blue (MB) solution in the presence of 50 mg CQDs/g-C3N4 for 30 min. As evidenced by electron density difference, the enhanced generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as the main mechanism for dye degradation was due to the enhanced surface area and increased number of reactive sites along with the existence of charge transfer channel, which was responsible for the dye degradation. Coupling CQDs with g-C3N4 nanosheets provides a new strategy of the construction of carbon-based nanocomposites towards promising piezo-catalytic applications. It also provides a new perspective for wastewater treatment on the dark environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-C3N4 nanosheets
Carbon quantum dots
Methylene blue
Piezoelectric catalytic

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62101391 ), the Key Project of the Education Department of Hubei Province (No. D20211702 ), the Science and Technology Planning Project of State Administration for Market Regulation (No. 2022MK084 ) and the High-value Intellectual Property Cultivation of Hubei Province (No. 2021 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematical illustration of the preparation of CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites.

UV–visible absorption spectrum (a), fluorescent properties (b), and TEM images of CQDs (c). Optical photos of g-C3N4 before and after CQDs incorporation (d). TEM image of g-C3N4 nanosheets (e) and CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (f).
XRD spectra (a), FTIR spectra (b), Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (c), XPS fully scanned spectra (d) together with C 1s spectrum (e) and N 1s spectrum (f) of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4.

Degradation of MB by CQDs/g-C3N4 with different contents of CQDs (a), under different stirring condition (b), at different cycle (c) and in desorption process (d), and PFM phase hysteresis loops and PFM amplitude butterfly loops of CQDs/g-C3N4 (e) and PFM images of CQDs/g-C3N4 (f).

Transient current response under vigorous stirring (a) and EIS curves (b) of the g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites; ESR spectra of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites for detection of DMPO-•O2− (c) and DMPO-•OH (d) under vigorous stirring; Lattice model of CQDs/g-C3N4 (e) schematics of the electron density distribution from the front view (f) ( Note: the yellow and blue isosurfaces represent the electron accumulation and depletion areas, respectively). Proposed catalysis mechanism of CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites: (g) the intrinsic piezoelectric characteristics of g-C3N4 and (h) CQDs acting as the electron medium. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-doped g-C3N4 nanocomposites with enhanced piezoelectric catalytic performance

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of charge transfer channel is a feasible way to enhance efficiency in catalysis applications. In this study, hydrothermally synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were deposited on g-C3N4 nanosheets via the coprecipitation calcination method to couple the piezoelectrical nature of g-C3N4 with the prominent charge transfer capability of CQDs, resulting in enhanced piezo-catalytic performance of the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Specifically, a 92.5% methylene blue degradation rate was achieved after constant stirring of the methylene blue (MB) solution in the presence of 50 mg CQDs/g-C3N4 for 30 min. As evidenced by electron density difference, the enhanced generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as the main mechanism for dye degradation was due to the enhanced surface area and increased number of reactive sites along with the existence of charge transfer channel, which was responsible for the dye degradation. Coupling CQDs with g-C3N4 nanosheets provides a new strategy of the construction of carbon-based nanocomposites towards promising piezo-catalytic applications. It also provides a new perspective for wastewater treatment on the dark environment.

In the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalyst system, the intrinsic piezoelectric characteristics of g-C3N4 induce the separation of electron-hole pairs while CQDs act as the electron medium to promote the carrier transfer, and their synergistic effect promotes the degradation of organic pollutants.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductive materials with photocatalytic reactivities utilizing solar energy to degrade organic pollutants are deemed to be one of the most promising technical solutions to environmental pollution and energy shortage, addressing threats to human health ecological environment as a result of water pollution [1–6]. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of these semiconductive materials remains at a relatively low level due to the recombination of photo-generated carriers and holes [7–10]. Generally, under the influence of coulomb force, the electron-hole pairs as generated by the excitation of semiconductor after light irradiation are prone to recombination, and only a small number of the carriers can escape from the recombination and migrate to the surface of the semiconductor for the subsequent photocatalytic reaction [11–14]. This insufficient utilization of the carriers leads to a low photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductors. Therefore, it is of great significance to discover novel catalytic materials with high electron-hole pair separation efficiency to facilitate the photocatalytic degradation of organic matter in wastewater [15,16].

In addition to solar energy, vibration action driven by clean energy sources like wind, tide and wave is one of the most available energy sources [17–21]. In piezoelectric catalysis, mechanical vibration causes the deformation of materials along with the generation of a built-in electric field [22–25]. As a result of the electric field force, electrons and holes are separated. Subsequently, some electrons escape from the dragging by Columba force and migrate to the surface of the materials. These electrons are further donated to the surrounding pollutants, initiating catalytic degradation of these pollutants [26–30]. Piezoelectric catalysis has attracted much attention due to its advantages of simple operation, low cost and non-toxicity [31–35]. Graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with a two-dimensional layered structure has a high piezoelectric coefficient, a large specific surface area and a high surface atomic ratio [36–40]. In addition, it has the advantages of simple preparation, environmental friendliness and suitability for large-scale production [41]. These features make g-C3N4 one of the most promising piezoelectric materials in recent years [42–45]. Nonetheless, further enhancing the carrier mobility of g-C3N4 to improve the piezoelectric conversion efficiency is still a big challenge.

Herein, we report a new method to realize improved catalytic activity through efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and rapid transfer of charge carriers. The CQDs/g-C3N4 piezoelectric catalysts have the capacity to convert environmental mechanical energy, tidal energy and wind energy into electrical energy. Compared to traditional photocatalysts, they could degrade the pollutants in a dark environment with great application potential in the future. The CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalysts were prepared via a simple high-temperature calcination method. CQDs with an average size of about 5 nm were loaded onto the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets. The CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts exhibit good catalytic activity in the degradation of methylene blue (MB) simply by stirring the MB solution without a light source in the presence of the as-prepared catalysts. The catalysts show excellent catalytic stability even after four cycles of photocatalytic MB degradation. Compared with pure g-C3N4, CQDs act as electron mediators bridging the g-C3N4 nanosheets and promote the migration of charge carriers to improve the catalytic efficiency of MB degradation. The degradation mechanism of CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts on organic pollutants is proposed to understand the role of CQDs in the photocatalytic system.
Citric acid and urea were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were synthesized by thermal polymerization, as shown schematically in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 10 g of urea was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water followed by drying in a crucible at 60 °C for 8 h for removing impurities. White crystalline solids were then obtained and transferred to a muffle furnace to calcine at 550 °C for 3 h under a heating rate of 15 °C/min. The as-prepared yellow powder was noted as g-C3N4.

Secondly, 15 g of citric acid and 5 g of urea were dissolved in 75 mL of deionized water. The solution was then transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for reaction at 180 °C for 5 h. A dark brown solution (CQDs solution) was obtained after subsequent collection of the supernatant by centrifugation.

Finally, 10 g of urea were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water followed by adding of CQDs solution with different concentrations. The mixed solution was then dried in a crucible at 60 °C for 8 h to obtain dark brown solids. The solids in the crucible were heated in a tube furnace at 550 °C for 3 h under a heating rate of 15 °C/min. In the end, a dark brown powder was obtained. The dark brown powder was coded as CQDs/g-C3N4.

UV–vis spectrophotometry of CQDs was characterized by SPECORD@210 Plus (Analytikjena). Structural properties of CQDs, g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 were measured by TEM (Tecnai G220S-TWIN, FEI Co., US) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV and 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained on an X-ray diffractometer (D/max 2500, Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation with the diffraction angle 2θ in the range of 10–70°. The FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 were collected on a TENSOR 27 instrument (Bruker Co., US) accompanied with the wavelength range of 4000-500 cm−1 and a resolution of 2 cm−1. The surface area and porosity were measured with a sorption analyzer (Micromeritics TriStar II 3020, Micromeritics Instrument Co., USA) at 77 K. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a PHI 5000C ESCA system with a Mg Kα source at 14.0 kV and 25 mA (PerkinElmer, USA).

The piezo-catalytic activity of CQDs/g-C3N4 was evaluated by the degradation of methylene blue (MB). 50 mg of CQDs/g-C3N4 with different CQDs contents was added into a 50 mL of aqueous solution with an initial methylene blue concentration of 5 mg/L. The solution was placed in dark and subject to magnet stirring using a 4 cm stirrer at a standard stirring speed of 250 rpm. Certain volumes were extracted from the suspension periodically for UV–vis spectrophotometric analysis. The amount of CQDs in the catalyst and stirring speed were varied to study the efficiency of piezo-catalysis. For the stability and reusability evaluation, the catalyst was gathered by centrifugation at the end of each run, and the gathered catalyst was washed with deionized water and dried for the next run.

The piezo-current response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were recorded on a CORRTEST CS310 electrochemical workstation with a standard three-electrode system. The working electrodes were prepared as follow: 10 mg catalyst and 50 uL Nafion were ultrasonically dispersed into 1 mL ethanol for 30 min to form a slurry. The slurry was spread onto an indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass with an area of 1 cm2 followed by natural drying. 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as electrolyte, while Pt foil and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. The EIS measurements were performed by the same system over a frequency range of 105-10-2 Hz with an AC amplitude of 0.27 V at open circuit potential.

The optical properties of the prepared CQDs were analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometry. The CQDs exhibit strong optical absorption in the UV region with tails extending into the visible light range, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The obtained CQDs show a broad absorption spectrum at 235 nm and 330 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the CC bond with sp2 hybridization and δ–π* transition of the CO bond, respectively [46]. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, a bright blue luminescence has been observed (Fig. 2b), revealing that the CQDs possess good fluorescent properties [47,48]. The morphology and size of the prepared CQDs were characterized by TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The TEM image in Fig. 2c shows that the CQDs are in round shape with an average diameter of about 4 nm. The HRTEM image (insert) shows that CQDs exhibit a crystalline structure with a minimum channel spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (101) lattice planes of graphite [49].

The color of the powder catalyst from yellow to dark brown when CQDs were incorporated into g-C3N4 (Fig. 2d), indicating the successful mix of CQDs with g-C3N4. The TEM image of g-C3N4 nanosheets shows a thin wrinkled nanosheet structure, which provides a larger specific surface area and numerous reactive sites (Fig. 2e). The TEM image of the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites shows that the CQDs are well anchored on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets (as shown the red circle in Fig. 2f).

The crystal structure of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites was characterized by XRD, as shown in Fig. 3 a. All the samples display two typical diffraction peaks, with one at around 13o attributing to trigonal N linkage of the tri-s-triazine (100) and the other one at 27o relating to the interplanar stacking of the conjugated aromatic planes of g-C3N4 (JCPDS NO. 87–1526) [50,51]. Notably, the crystal phase of g-C3N4 shows no significant change after coupling with CQDs, and this might be due to the low loading amount of CQDs.

The FTIR spectra of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 show a similar pattern as shown in Fig. 3b. The band at 3000–3400 cm−1 is related to the N–H stretching and the O–H group from water molecules on the surface. The Peaks at 1200–1700 cm−1 and 700–900 cm−1 correspond to the CN stretching vibrations and the vibration modes of triazine units, respectively [52,53]. It can be inferred that the original structure of g-C3N4 shows no evident change after loading a small amount of CQDs, and this finding is in consistence with the XRD results.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 3c. The physical adsorption isotherms of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 in the range of 0.8-1.0 P/P0 present the typical IV with a distinct H1 hysteresis loop, indicating the existence of mesoporous structure. The surface area of the CQDs/g-C3N4 is calculated to be 65.34 m2/g, which is significantly larger than that of g-C3N4 (36.01 m2/g). The higher surface area of the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites means more adsorption and active sites with enhanced adsorption and catalytic capacity [54,55]. In addition, the pore size distribution of the catalysts in the inset of Fig. 3c further illustrates the mesoporous structure of CQDs/g-C3N4.

Furthermore, the chemical composition of above samples was further determined by XPS. The survey spectra of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 clearly show C, N, and O elements (Fig. 3d). After adding CQDs, the peaks of C and O have been more prominent, indicating that CQDs affect the element contents of the nanocomposites. The change of functional groups in the samples was further examined by high-resolution XPS spectroscopy. The C 1s spectrum of g-C3N4 (Fig. 3e) presents two peaks at 284.6 eV and 288.1 eV, attributing to C–C coordination of the surface adventitious carbon and sp2-hybridized carbon in NC–N coordination, respectively [56,57]. For the CQDs/g-C3N4, a new peak at 286.3 eV assigning to the C–OH can be observed, indicating the interactions between hydroxyl-rich CQDs and g-C3N4. The high-resolution spectra of N 1s (Fig. 3f) show three peaks at 398.5 eV, 400.0 eV and 401.1 eV, corresponding to sp2-bonded nitrogen in triazine rings (CN–C), tertiary nitrogen in N-(C)3 and amino groups with hydrogen (N–H), respectively [58,59]. In addition, the positions of these peaks remain the same after the introduction of CQDs, indicating that CQDs is not binding to g-C3N4 through N containing chemical bonds. These results indicate that CQDs have been successfully loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 via C–O bond.

The catalytic activity of CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalysts was evaluated via degradation of methylene blue (MB). The composite catalysts were dispersed into the MB solution for photocatalyst performance test. In order to avoid the influence of light on the dye degradation process, magnetic stirring was carried out in a completely dark space. The degradation degree of MB solution was determined by measuring its concentration via UV–vis spectrophotometry at regular time intervals. In order to explore the role of CQDs in the composite catalysts, the influence of the amount of CQDs on the catalytic performance of degradation of MB was investigated. As seen from Fig. 4 a, the concentration MB solution was declined by approximately 50% after the dye solution had been subjected to constant stirring for 30 min in the presence of pure g-C3N4. The intrinsic piezoelectricity of g-C3N4 nanosheets due to the large amount of polar tris-s-triazine structures between them has resulted in the decline in MB concentration. The degradation efficiency of MB in all CQDs/g-C3N4 samples is higher than that of pure g-C3N4, demonstrating that the introduction of CQDs can significantly improve the catalytic activity of the g-C3N4. Among them, the catalytic efficiency of MB by adding 100 uL CQDs reached to 90%, which was much better than that of pure g-C3N4 (50%). The enhanced degradation efficiency after loading CQDs is mainly due to the existence of charge transfer channel to avoid rapid recombination of charge-hole pairs. Besides, loading CQDs has greatly enhanced the BET surface area of the catalyst with numerous active sites. Moreover, the enhanced surface roughness of g-C3N4 after loading CQDs greatly promotes the degradation efficiency. Interestingly, the catalytic efficiency did not increase with the increase of the loading amount of CQDs. The reason for this is probably that the presence of high content CQDs may reduce the pressure applied to the g-C3N4, leading to the declining of catalytic activity.

Stirring condition is another important factor affecting the degradation of MB. The influence of stirring speed on the catalytic performance of the CQDs/g-C3N4 for MB degradation was investigated. Without stirring, only 20% MB of solution was adsorbed or degraded (see Fig. 4b). The CQDs/g-C3N4 adsorbs a small amount of MB molecules through physical adsorption in static condition, which results in the slight decline in the concentration of MB solution. With the increase of stirring speed, the efficiency of MB degradation was greatly improved, as seen from Fig. 4b. The enhanced efficiency of MB degradation can be due to the enhanced kinetic energy of MB dye molecules resulting in an increased amount of adsorption to the CQDs/g-C3N4, the friction of the nanosheet promoting the surface charge and the accelerated charge transfer of CQDs/g-C3N4.

Stability is an important factor to measure the quality of catalysts. The cyclic experiment of catalytic degradation of MB by the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalysts was conducted to study their stability, as shown the results in Fig. 4c. The composite catalysts can still maintain high catalytic performance after 4 cycles, indicating that the excellent cyclic stability of the catalysts.

In order to confirm that the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalysts can effectively degrade most MB dyes rather than physical adsorption, desorption experiments was carried out and the results are shown in Fig. 4d. The concentration of MB solution dropped from 100% to 7.5% after adding the CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts for 30 min. The catalyst powder was collected by centrifugation and filtration followed by washing with deionized water under ultrasound for 1 h. The concentration of MB in the upper liquid was 5.8% after centrifugation. The results have proven that the catalysts can degrade most of the MB dye molecules by stirring without a light source.

The piezo-response force microscopy (PFM) was employed to explore the intrinsic reason for the piezo-catalytic performance of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4. During the experiment, the reversible electric field was simultaneously applied to generate local vibration to the samples for acquiring the piezo-response. As shown in Fig. 4e and Figs. S1a–b, the g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 exhibit typical square phase hysteresis loops and butterfly loops during the PFM measurement under a reversal electric field of –10 V to 10 V. The phase angle change was 180° corresponding to the reversing of the 10 V electric field, indicating the piezoelectric response of the g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs/g-C3N4 [60,61]. Meanwhile, the piezoelectric coefficient (d33 value) was calculated by fitting the PFM phase hysteresis loops, reflecting the ratio of strain change to electric field intensity change. The d33 value of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 were 47.8 and 51.2 p.m./V, respectively, demonstrating that the loading of CQDs has enhanced the piezoelectricity of g-C3N4. Fig. 4f and Fig. S1c exhibits the topographical image and the corresponding PFM phase image of CQDs/g-C3N4. They present an inhomogeneous surface potential distribution of CQDs/g-C3N4, indicating that the free electrons and holes have transferred in opposite directions with good piezoelectricity.

Transient current responses and electrochemical impediments were measured in order to understand the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs under vigorous stirring. In the i-t curves (Fig. 5 a), the current density of CQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalysts is significantly higher than pure g-C3N4, suggesting a distinct enhancement of the electron transfer rate under vigorous stirring. The CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalysts show a smaller arc radius than that of pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 5b), indicating its lower charge-transfer resistance and therefore more effective separation and transfer of the electron-hole pairs at the heterojunction interface.

To determine the main active species in this catalytic process under vigorous stirring, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique using 5,5-dimethy-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trapper was carried out. As shown in Fig. 5c–d, typical spin patterns of both DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − can be observed from the ESR spectra, indicating the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) due to the piezoelectricity of CQDs/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 nanosheets. Specifically, the intensity of DMPO-•OH signal for the CQDs/g-C3N4 is obviously stronger than that of g-C3N4 under the same stirring catalytic system. Loading of CQDs resulted in an enhanced peak intensity of DMPO-•OH due to more hydroxyl radical production. Furthermore, four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 were found. Likewise, the DMPO-•O2 − over g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 was observed under vigorous stirring (Fig. 5c), and its peak intensity on CQDs/g-C3N4 was much stronger than that on g-C3N4, indicating that CQDs coupling can greatly improve the molecular oxygen activation capacity of CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. The ESR spectrum results are in good consistence with the degradation efficiency.

The interaction and synergistic effect between CQDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets were calculated by first principle theory (FPT), as shown in Fig. 5e–f. It is notable that an electron accumulation area has been formed underneath the planer structure of CQDs. This is consistent with an electron depletion area formed right above the planer structure of g-C3N4 unit, corresponding to positively charged atoms. The alignment in the charge density distribution indicates the possible electron transfer from the positively charged section in g-C3N4 side to the electron accumulation section in the CQDs side. Similar electron transfer tendencies have been reported in literature [62,63], indicating the promising electron donor property of g-C3N4 when coupling with other semiconductors or 1D/2D graphene structures, i.e., CQDs. In a word, the transient photocurrent response, EIS curves and density functional theory demonstrated that CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites have an outstanding coupling effect to promote the separation and transfer efficiency of charge carriers.

Based on the above experimental results and FPT calculation, the possible catalytic mechanism of as-prepared CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalysts was deduced. Basically, the electron source that triggers the catalysis of the organic compounds (such as MB) can be attributed to the piezoelectric nature of g-C3N4. More specifically, when the catalysts are agitated by the friction such as the stirring action, the electrons in the nitrogen atoms escape from the nucleus and become free electrons, leaving empty spaces as holes. Due to the good piezoelectric properties of g-C3N4, the polarized electric field as generated by the directional migration of polarized charges in piezoelectric materials can effectively induce the electrons and holes to move in opposite directions (Fig. 5g). The excited electrons react with oxygen to form superoxide anion (•O2-), and the holes react with H2O to yield hydroxyl radicals (•OH). The CQDs on the surface of g-C3N4 can act as efficient charge-transfer channels to facilitate charge-carrier transfer (Fig. 5h). All in all, in the CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite catalyst system, the intrinsic piezoelectric characteristics of g-C3N4 induce the separation of electron-hole pairs while CQDs act as the electron medium to promote the carrier transfer, and their synergistic effect promotes the degradation of organic pollutants.

Hydrothermally synthesized CQDs were coupled with g-C3N4 via the coprecipitation calcination method. The deposition of CQDs on the g-C3N4 nanosheets led to a larger surface area with increased reactive sites on the as-prepared CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. A synergistic charge-transfer effect was confirmed by the appearance of electron accumulation area in the electron density difference map as calculated via FPT with VASP. The coupling of CQDs and g-C3N4 presented enhanced piezo-catalytic reactivity of g-C3N4, resulting in a 92.5% degradation rate of methylene blue after constant stirring of an MB solution in the presence of 50 mg CQDs/g-C3N4 for 30 min. Possible causes for this enhanced piezo-catalytic nature of CQDs/g-C3N4 were found to be the evident inhomogeneous surface potential distribution as observed from its PFM image and the increase in the current density and number of ROS as triggered by the shearing agitation of the nanocomposites. Overall, the intrinsic piezoelectric nature of g-C3N4 induced the electron-hole pair separation while CQDs act as the charge transfer passage to facilitate the transfer of piezoelectrically generated electrons from the catalyst to the surrounding organic compounds. This coupling strategy of CQDs/g-C3N4 provides insights into the development of carbon-based nanocomposites with promising piezo-catalytic applications. It also provides a new perspective for wastewater treatment in dark environment.

Changwang Yan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhong Zhao: Methodology, Investigation. Wanhui Jin: Investigation. Qian Yu: Methodology. Jing Yu: Methodology. Jianhua Ran: Investigation. Shuguang Bi: Investigation. Deshan Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision. Daiqi Li: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Guangming Cai: Investigation. Xin Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62101391), the Key Project of the Education Department of Hubei Province (No. D20211702), the Science and Technology Planning Project of State Administration for Market Regulation (No. 2022MK084) and the High-value Intellectual Property Cultivation of Hubei Province (No. 2021).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.coco.2022.101466.
